note_data.title: Accounting rules
keyPoints: **Accounting concepts** are fundamental principles that guide the preparation of financial statements
The **business entity concept** states that the business is separate from the owner and business transactions are recorded separately from personal transactions
The **money measurement concept** means only transactions that can be measured in monetary terms are recorded in the books of accounts
The **going concern concept** assumes that the business will continue to operate for the foreseeable future
The **accruals concept** records income when earned and expenses when incurred, regardless of when cash is received or paid
The **consistency concept** requires the same accounting methods to be used from one period to another
The **prudence concept** means income is only recorded when realized but all known losses and expenses are recorded
**Accounting conventions** are practices that have evolved over time to solve practical accounting problems
The **materiality convention** states that items of little value need not be treated with strict theoretical correctness

definitions.definition: The business is treated as separate from the owner and business transactions are recorded separately from personal transactions of the owner
Income is recorded when earned and expenses are recorded when incurred, regardless of when cash is received or paid
The assumption that the business will continue to operate for the foreseeable future
Income is only recorded when it is certain to be received but all known losses and expenses are recorded immediately
The same accounting methods and policies should be used from one accounting period to another

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the name of the concept/convention only. No explanation needed.
Give the definition + practical example using 'This means that...' or 'For example...'
Name the specific accounting concept being applied in the given scenario

modelAnswer.question: Explain the accruals concept and give one example of how it is applied. (3 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: The accruals concept means that income is recorded when earned and expenses are recorded when incurred, regardless of when cash is received or paid. For example, if rent is owed for December but paid in January, the rent expense is still recorded in December's accounts because this is when the expense was incurred.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Expenses recorded when incurred/regardless of cash movement

commonMistakes: Never confuse accruals concept with cash accounting - accruals ignores when cash is received/paid
Students often mix up prudence concept with other concepts - prudence only records certain income but all known expenses
Don't say 'matching concept' - the correct term is 'accruals concept' in Cambridge O Level
Never give personal examples when explaining business entity concept - always use business transactions vs personal transactions
Students forget that going concern affects asset valuation - assets valued at cost not break-up value
examinerTips: Accounting concepts appear in Paper 1 MCQ and Paper 2 every year - usually 4-6 marks total
Always give practical business examples when explaining concepts for full marks
Questions often ask which concept is being applied in a scenario - read carefully
Learn the exact definitions - examiners look for key phrases like 'when earned' and 'when incurred'
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: No specific diagram required for this topic, but create a concept map showing the relationship between different accounting concepts and their applications
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — they asked students to identify which accounting concept requires assets to be recorded at cost price
O/N 2022 P2 Q4 (8 marks) tested the dual aspect concept by asking candidates to show how a business transaction affects two accounts simultaneously
M/J 2022 P1 Q18 (1 mark) focused on the consistency concept, asking why a business should use the same depreciation method each year
O/N 2021 P2 Q6 (6 marks) required students to explain how the prudence concept affects the valuation of inventory and provision for doubtful debts

workedExample.question: Ahmed's business purchased a delivery van for $15,000 cash. Explain how this transaction demonstrates the dual aspect concept and show the accounting entries. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify that dual aspect means every transaction affects two accounts with equal amounts
Step 2: Recognize that purchasing a van increases Motor Vehicles (asset) by $15,000
Step 3: Identify that paying cash decreases Bank/Cash (asset) by $15,000
Step 4: Show the accounting entries: Dr Motor Vehicles $15,000, Cr Bank $15,000
workedExample.answer: The dual aspect concept states that every business transaction has two effects of equal value. When Ahmed purchases the van for $15,000 cash: (1) Motor Vehicles account increases by $15,000 (asset acquired), (2) Bank account decreases by $15,000 (asset given up). Accounting entries: Dr Motor Vehicles $15,000, Cr Bank $15,000. This demonstrates dual aspect as both sides equal $15,000.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for defining dual aspect concept, 1 mark for identifying increase in Motor Vehicles, 1 mark for identifying decrease in Bank, 1 mark for correct accounting entries

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the money measurement concept with historical cost concept' — always specify that money measurement means only monetary transactions are recorded, not the valuation method
Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates often failed to give practical examples when explaining concepts' — always provide a business example when defining accounting concepts like materiality or prudence

Examiner Report 2021: 'Students incorrectly stated that dual aspect means having two books of accounts' — remember that dual aspect refers to every transaction affecting two accounts with equal amounts
note_data.keyDiagram: Draw the Accounting Equation triangle showing Assets at the top, with Capital and Liabilities at the base corners. Include arrows showing how the dual aspect concept maintains the equation balance (Assets = Capital + Liabilities) after every transaction. Add example transactions around the triangle showing increases/decreases to demonstrate how changes in one element require balancing changes elsewhere.
note_data.quickRecap: Accounting concepts are fundamental rules that guide how financial transactions are recorded and presented. Key concepts include business entity (business separate from owner), dual aspect (every transaction affects two accounts equally), money measurement (only monetary transactions recorded), and historical cost (assets recorded at purchase price). These concepts ensure consistency, reliability and comparability of financial statements across different businesses and time periods.
